text.title: Value Stream Management — Framework and Metrics
text.summary: VSM principles from the Flow Framework, DORA four-tuple, VSM + DORA combination pattern, and the implementation sequence.

section.title: Value Stream Mapping Fundamentals

rule.statement: Map from "user filed request" to "user received value" — not from "ticket created" to "merged"; customer-visible time is the only honest measurement boundary.
rule.rationale: Internal pipeline metrics (commit-to-deploy) can look elite while the customer waits weeks for spec, design, and compliance stages that precede the first commit. Only customer-anchored boundaries reveal actual value flow.

section.p: VSM originated from lean manufacturing; Mik Kersten (Project to Product, 2018) adapted it to software with the Flow Framework. It identifies bottlenecks outside the software team — the stages that DevOps automation cannot reach.

list.item: Lead Time: time from accepting work to customer handoff — end-to-end flow. The primary metric.
Cycle Time: time within a single process stage — process efficiency. Used to locate the constraint.
Throughput: items completed per time period — delivery rate.
WIP: work items in progress simultaneously — per Little's Law, WIP = Throughput × Cycle Time.
%C/A (Complete and Accurate): percentage received by downstream stage without rework needed — quality of handoffs.

rule.statement: Split flow data by work type (feature, defect, risk, debt) — single-number flow metrics hide trade-offs.
rule.rationale: A team shipping 100% defect work has high throughput but zero feature delivery; aggregating disguises this. Mik Kersten's four-type split is the minimum granularity.

list.item: Deployment Frequency: how often code deploys to production. Elite = multiple per day.
Change Lead Time: commit to production time. Elite = under 1 hour.
Change Failure Rate: percentage of deployments causing a failure. Elite = under 5%.
Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR): time to restore service after failure. Elite = under 1 hour.

rule.statement: Never optimize one DORA metric in isolation — always pair DF with CFR; high DF + high CFR is a negative trade-off.
rule.rationale: Deployment frequency can be gamed by deploying trivial changes; the constraint is value delivery per deploy, not deploys per day in isolation.

rule.statement: Do not publish "Elite DORA" status without trend context — the label is meaningless and will be gamed by teams optimizing for the scorecard.
rule.rationale: DORA levels were designed as benchmarks for improvement, not badges. Publishing static levels without trend data invites Goodhart's Law to destroy the signal.

list.item: 1. Instrument: connect Jira/Linear, GitHub, deploy logs, PagerDuty, Zendesk via ID links — commit timestamps are the foundation.
2. Map: trace one work item end-to-end; identify the stages; measure Lead Time and Cycle Time per stage.
3. Measure DORA: daily collection on git + deploy logs.
4. Identify constraint: apply Little's Law; highest WIP × Cycle Time stage is the system bottleneck (Theory of Constraints).
5. Experiment: run one targeted experiment on the constraint; measure its effect on Lead Time.
6. Monitor: quarterly VSM refresh; refresh the map; do not assume the stream stays static.

antipattern.description: VSM workshop that produces a poster but no telemetry instrumentation.
antipattern.reason: Without instrumentation, the VSM map decays into fiction within months as the stream evolves. Map and metric collection must happen simultaneously.
